title: Grounded Satirical Generation with RAG
research_questions: To what extent are the generated definitions humorous and politically meaningful?
To what extent is successful satire generation dependent on cultural context?
How does the choice of the candidate word affect the quality of the generated definitions?
Does RAG improve the quality of generated satirical definitions?
Can LLMs serve as reliable evaluators of satirical content?

datasets_used.name: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

methods_proposed.name: RAG-based grounded satire generation pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The pipeline scrapes English-language Yle news, filters by timestamp and sentiment, uses topic modeling to select candidate words, retrieves up to 3 semantically relevant news snippets, and prompts Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct to generate short satirical dictionary definitions grounded only in the retrieved context.

methods_proposed.name: Task-specific satire evaluation framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper operationalizes satire evaluation with two 1-to-5 Likert questions, Q1: “Is it funny?” and Q2: “Is it political?”, and applies the same rubric to human annotators and LLM judges.

methods_proposed.name: Web application for grounded satire generation
methods_proposed.summary: The authors present a local web application that can generate definitions and search relevant news for user input, including keywords derived from topic modeling.

humor_theories.detail: The paper adopts the Cambridge Dictionary definition of satire as criticizing people or ideas in a humorous way, especially to make a political point, and uses this to define the two evaluation dimensions: humor and political significance.

evaluation_methods.detail: Six annotators evaluated 100 randomly shuffled definitions in a blind setup; half of the annotators were Finnish and half came from different cultural backgrounds, and all rated humor and political relevance on 1-to-5 Likert scales.
Five instruction-tuned open-weight models of comparable size scored the same definitions using the same annotation guidelines as the human annotators.

The paper reports Krippendorff’s α for inter-annotator agreement, Mann-Whitney U tests for cultural background and word-selection comparisons, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for RAG vs. non-RAG definitions, and Spearman correlation ρ with 95% confidence intervals for LLM-human agreement.

headline_results.finding: Human annotators perceived the generated definitions as more political than funny.
headline_results.value: Funny M=1.98, SD=1.06; 40% of the annotations receiving a score of 1; Political M=2.53, SD=1.55
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

headline_results.finding: Inter-annotator agreement was very low for humor and higher, but still cautious, for political relevance.
headline_results.value: All: Humor 0.070, Politics 0.514; Finnish: Humor 0.053, Politics 0.646; International: Humor 0.183, Politics 0.490
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

headline_results.finding: Cultural background did not have a statistically significant effect on ratings.
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

headline_results.finding: Topic-modeled candidate words led to definitions perceived as more political, but not funnier, than random words.
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

headline_results.finding: RAG improved political relevance but did not clearly improve humor.
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

headline_results.finding: Aya-Expanse-8B achieved the highest overall correlation with human judgments among the LLM judges, especially for political relevance.
headline_results.metric: Spearman correlation ρ with human mean scores; mean score M ± SD
headline_results.dataset: Annotated corpus of generated satirical dictionary definitions

key_findings: The generated satirical dictionary definitions were rated low on humor but somewhat higher on political relevance.
Finnish and international annotators did not differ significantly in their ratings, despite the paper’s expectation that satire would depend on cultural context.
Topic-based word selection improved perceived political relevance but not perceived humor.
RAG improved perceived political relevance compared with the non-RAG baseline, but did not yield clear gains in humor generation.
LLM judges correlated strongly with humans on political relevance but poorly on humor, with Aya-Expanse-8B performing best overall.
Human satire annotation was difficult: humor agreement was very low, and political agreement remained below commonly used thresholds for strong reliability.
limitations: The current pipeline is limited to English news from the Finnish public broadcaster Yle.
The authors state that future work should extend the approach to additional languages and sources.
The authors state that future work should explore alternative evaluation methods for a broader and more robust assessment of system performance.
Inter-annotator agreement for humor was very low, and political agreement was below commonly used thresholds for strong reliability.
safety_ethics_notes: To reduce the risk of offensive or disturbing satire, the pipeline applies sentiment analysis to filter out overly negative news, treating sentiment as a proxy for how sensitive or severe a news topic may be; the authors set the sentiment-analysis threshold to one and discard news with a label lower than one.

artifacts.other: The paper presents a local web application for generating definitions and searching relevant news; it states that the repository of the application will be made public upon acceptance.
